mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the full scope of section B: simple machines (B.1), machine mechanisms via gears, cams, and linkages (B.2, B.3), and the concept of mechanical advantage — the foundational article for the machines-and-components portion of the outline.

B.1 lists wheel and axle as one of the five simple machines; the article covers the wheel-and-axle principle, rolling friction reduction, and flywheel function, connecting to both B.1 and B.3.d (flywheels).

Covers the prehistoric development of hand tools (A.1) from stone through metal, and the taxonomy of hand-operated implements (A.2) — the article's core subject matter matches section A's historical and typological coverage of hand tools.
B.1 names the lever, wedge, wheel and axle, pulley, and screw as simple machines — exactly the six classical devices this article describes, including their mechanical advantage principles.

A.2.b lists cutting tools as a basic hand tool type; the knife is humanity's oldest cutting implement and the article covers its evolution from Oldowan stone blades through metal types — direct coverage of this path.

B.1 names the lever as one of the five simple machines; the article covers all three lever classes, mechanical advantage ratios, and compound levers, with direct relevance also to B.2's machine mechanisms that transmit and amplify force.

D.3 explicitly names robots and D.4 names computer-integrated manufacturing; the article covers robot design, manipulation, sensing, and manufacturing applications — direct coverage of both computer-aided machining paths.

D.1 names computer numerical control, D.2 names CADCAM, D.4 names computer-integrated manufacturing — the article covers the full history and architecture of computers that underpin all these computer-aided machining paths.

A.1 covers the development of metal tools; C.1 addresses the history of machine tools — the Industrial Revolution introduced mechanized production and machine tools as its core technological contribution, directly covering both paths.

Archimedes formulated the law of the lever and designed compound pulleys — directly relevant to B.1 (simple machines) and B.2 (machine mechanisms); the article covers his statics principles and engineering devices substantively.

A.1 covers hand tool history from Paleolithic stone through metal; C.1 addresses the history of machine tools — this article traces that full arc from Stone Age lithics through Industrial Revolution machine tools and modern manufacturing.

A.1 explicitly names Paleolithic and Neolithic stone tools as the starting point for hand tool history; this article is the dedicated source on that period, covering Oldowan through Neolithic tool development in detail.

A.1 covers the development of metal tools — the shift from bronze to iron tools is a pivotal transition in tool history, and the article covers iron metallurgy and steelmaking that enabled precision metal tools and machine components.

Mechanical engineering is the discipline governing machine design (B), machine tool operation (C), and CAD/CAM systems (D); the article covers all these domains as core competencies of the field including mechatronics and robotics.

A.1 covers the development of metal tools; C.2 and C.3 concern metal-cutting operations and machine tools — metallurgy provides the materials science governing metal properties, workability, and tool material selection for all three paths.

Machine tools (C) are the core of manufacturing; CADCAM (D.2) and computer-integrated manufacturing (D.4) are explicitly manufacturing technologies — the article covers fabrication, assembly, and machining across industrial history.

A.1 addresses the development of metal tools; the Bronze Age is defined by bronze tool and weapon manufacture, and the article covers the metallurgical developments enabling the first widespread metal tools used across construction and agriculture.

A.1 names Paleolithic and Neolithic stone tools as the starting history of hand tools; prehistory spans exactly this period and the article organizes human prehistory by the three-age system centered on tool materials.

B.1 (simple machines) and B.2 (machine mechanisms) depend on force, mechanical advantage, and torque — the article covers F=ma, torque as rotational analogue, and friction forces foundational to understanding how levers, gears, and linkages transmit force.

Engineering is the encompassing discipline for machine design (B), machine tool operation (C), and computer-aided manufacturing (D); the article covers engineering methodology and design processes spanning all these subsections.
